story:
The server in the orange apron offered more coffee; Cath, voice unsteady, declined. Fog blurred Waterloo Bridge beyond the café window. The quiet morning and the café’s warmth held her while the Thames sank into white. She had come up from the Tube through empty streets. Tourists still slept; office workers had already taken their seats in bright “creative-flow” spaces.

Yesterday she had led onboarding in one of those rooms. The new hires chirped like a flock of birds. Cath felt their glances until a young woman, blushing, raised a hand: “Aren’t you Catherine Lester?” Cath smiled—her therapist prescribed ten smiles a day; she typically managed five. “You’re wondering why a prima ballerina works in HR,” she said lightly. An injury had ended her career; she liked working with people; HR made sense. If there were no more questions, her colleague Viv would continue. Later, Viv said Cath seemed to be “processing her tragedy” and reminded her to ask for help. Cath, who wanted to bite off Viv’s head, forced another practiced smile.

The night before she had drunk herself to stillness, clutching a bottle of whiskey and the last letter from Mark, delivered more than a year ago. He had loved paper, the feel of an envelope, the neatness of a military address. “We’re almost done here and coming back in a month,” he wrote. “Till we meet again, my love, on Waterloo Bridge.” They both loved the old film. Finishing her coffee now, Cath toyed with the gold links of the bracelet Mark had given her for their first and last anniversary. Their second would have been today.

That morning, in the letterbox she checked every day as if ritual could bend time, she had found an army postcard: “Meet me on Waterloo Bridge,” written in Mark’s precise hand—though he had been dead for over a year. She tried to remember if she had taken her pills yesterday. Probably not. She had been drinking. Today? Time felt slippery. The postcard itself had vanished from her hands, as if it had never existed. The fog outside thickened, soft and blank.

She put on her coat and stepped into the hushed streets. The bridge rose ahead, swallowed in white. At the crest, the world went silent. “I knew you’d come,” came a whisper that was soft and intimate, like a memory. “I’m waiting for you.” Grief pried open the past. She moved toward the railing, seeking the voice that floated away like water. In the fog she reached for what wasn’t there—love, the letter, the neat handwriting, the promise to meet on this place they had named together.

The cold came up from the river. The city lay muted at her back; the film in her mind replayed its final scene. She leaned into the white, eyes searching the mist for Mark’s shape, for proof that love could step out of memory. There was only fog and the pull of the river below. Haunted by loss, dulled by whiskey, and unmoored from the routine that steadied her, she moved past the safe line, a dangerous mistake born of pain and hallucination. The voice dissolved. The water waited. The morning held its breath.
